title: Matrices: determinants, inverses and applications
summary: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers that behaves like a single object you can add, scale and multiply, and this lesson builds the full HL 1.6 toolkit around it. It covers the matrix operations you must be fluent in, addition, scalar multiplication and multiplication, together with the conformability rule that decides when a product even exists; the identity matrix and its role; the determinant of a 2x2 matrix (by hand) and of a 3x3 matrix (by GDC); the inverse of a 2x2 matrix from the formula-booklet formula and of a 3x3 from the GDC; the central application of writing a linear system as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ and solving it as $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$; and further applications such as encoding messages and representing geometric transformations as matrices. Because matrices sit on Paper 1 as well as Paper 2, the emphasis is on getting the order of multiplication right, knowing when a matrix is singular, and showing the method our marking engine rewards. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way that engine awards the marks.
learningObjectives: Add, scalar-multiply and multiply matrices, and use the conformability rule to decide when a product is defined and what its order will be.
State the role of the identity matrix and calculate the determinant of a 2x2 matrix by hand and of a 3x3 matrix using the GDC.
Find the inverse of a 2x2 matrix using the formula and of a 3x3 matrix using the GDC, and recognise a singular matrix from a zero determinant.
Represent a system of linear equations as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ and solve it as $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, using the GDC for 3x3 systems.
Apply matrices to real contexts such as encoding messages and geometric transformations, and present working so method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.

simpleExplanation.title: The Matrix Master Key
simpleExplanation.summary: A matrix packages a whole block of numbers into one object you can add, scale and multiply. Its determinant is a single number that tells you whether the matrix can be 'undone', and its inverse is the master key that unlocks a whole system of equations in one step.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a secret message written as a column of numbers. To encode it you multiply by a secret 'key' matrix, scrambling the numbers into nonsense. Your friend decodes it by multiplying the scramble by the inverse key, which puts the original numbers back. The determinant is a quick health-check on the key: if it is zero, no inverse exists, so the code can never be undone uniquely. Solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ works the same way, the inverse of the coefficient matrix is the key that reveals the unknowns.
steps: Check the shapes. Before multiplying two matrices, confirm they are conformable, the number of columns of the first must equal the number of rows of the second, and the product inherits the outer dimensions.
Find the determinant. For a 2x2 matrix use $ad-bc$ by hand; for a 3x3 use the GDC. If it is zero the matrix is singular and has no inverse.
Find the inverse. For a 2x2 use the formula-booklet formula; for a 3x3 go straight to the GDC. Multiplying a matrix by its inverse gives the identity.
Solve the system. Write the equations as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$, then compute $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ (order matters), reading the answer straight off the GDC for a 3x3 system.

flashcards.back: To form $AB$ the number of columns of $A$ must equal the number of rows of $B$. An $(m \times n)$ matrix times an $(n \times p)$ matrix gives an $(m \times p)$ matrix. If the inner dimensions disagree, the product is undefined.

No. In general $AB \neq BA$; sometimes only one of the two products is even defined. Always preserve the order in which matrices are written, this is why we pre-multiply to solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$.

A square matrix with 1s on the leading diagonal and 0s elsewhere. It is the 'do nothing' matrix: $AI = IA = A$ for any conformable $A$. The $2 \times 2$ identity is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
For $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, $\det A = ad - bc$. It is a scalar (a single number), not a matrix, and it decides whether $A$ has an inverse.
In AI HL you evaluate a $3 \times 3$ determinant with the GDC, not by hand. You still need to know what it tells you: $\det A = 0$ means singular (no inverse, no unique solution).

Singular: $\det A = 0$, so $A$ has NO inverse. Non-singular: $\det A \neq 0$, so $A^{-1}$ exists and is unique. Only non-singular coefficient matrices give a unique solution to $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$.
$A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$: swap the leading diagonal, negate the other diagonal, divide by the determinant. This formula is in the formula booklet.
Use the GDC. Enter the matrix and apply the inverse (x^{-1}) key. You are never expected to invert a $3 \times 3$ by hand in AI HL; the marks are for setting up and interpreting, not for grinding.
$A^{-1}$ is the matrix with $AA^{-1} = A^{-1}A = I$. Multiplying by the inverse 'undoes' the matrix, exactly as dividing undoes multiplying for ordinary numbers.

flashcards.front: Matrix form of a linear system
flashcards.back: A system of linear equations is written $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, where $A$ holds the coefficients, $\mathbf{x}$ is the column of unknowns and $\mathbf{b}$ is the column of constants. Line up the variables in the same order in every equation before reading off $A$.

Pre-multiply by $A^{-1}$: $A^{-1}A\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, so $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. The inverse must go on the LEFT of $\mathbf{b}$, and a unique solution exists only when $\det A \neq 0$.

Because multiplication is not commutative, $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, never $\mathbf{b}A^{-1}$. In fact $\mathbf{b}A^{-1}$ is not even conformable when $\mathbf{b}$ is a column, so the wrong order is a genuine error, not a stylistic choice.
Forgetting the scalar $\frac{1}{ad-bc}$: candidates swap and negate the entries but leave out the division by the determinant. No division means the answer is not an inverse, an easy mark to lose.

Multiplying a position vector by a matrix maps it to a new point, so matrices encode geometric transformations (rotations, reflections, enlargements, shears). Combining transformations corresponds to multiplying their matrices, in reverse order of application.

quickCheck.prompt: For two matrices $A$ and $B$ for which both products exist, which statement is always true?
options: $AB$ and $BA$ always have the same dimensions

quickCheck.prompt: The product $AB$ of an $(2 \times 3)$ matrix $A$ and a $(3 \times 4)$ matrix $B$ is:
The determinant of $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$ is:
To solve $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ for the column vector $\mathbf{x}$, you compute:
A square matrix $A$ has $\det A = 0$. This means:
options: $A$ is singular and has no inverse
quickCheck.answer: $A$ is singular and has no inverse

faq.q: When is a matrix product actually defined?
faq.a: Only when the matrices are conformable: the number of columns of the first must equal the number of rows of the second. An $(m \times n)$ times an $(n \times p)$ gives an $(m \times p)$ result; if the two inner numbers disagree the product simply does not exist. This is why checking shapes first saves you from writing down a product that cannot be formed.

faq.q: Why is the solution $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ and not $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}A^{-1}$?
faq.a: Matrix multiplication is not commutative, so order matters. Starting from $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, pre-multiplying both sides by $A^{-1}$ gives $A^{-1}A\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, and since $A^{-1}A = I$ and $I\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}$ this simplifies to $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Writing $\mathbf{b}A^{-1}$ reverses the order and, with $\mathbf{b}$ a column vector, is not even conformable, so it is wrong, not just unconventional.

faq.q: Do I need to invert a 3x3 matrix by hand?
faq.a: No. In AI HL you invert a $2 \times 2$ matrix using the formula-booklet formula, and you use the GDC for anything larger, including every $3 \times 3$. The skill being examined is setting the problem up as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ and driving the calculator, not performing lengthy cofactor arithmetic by hand.

faq.q: What does a zero determinant mean for a system of equations?
faq.a: If $\det A = 0$ the coefficient matrix is singular and has no inverse, so $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has no unique solution, it either has no solutions or infinitely many. Geometrically the equations describe planes (or lines) that fail to meet in a single point. On the GDC this shows up as an error when you try to compute $A^{-1}$ or $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

faq.q: How are matrices used for encoding and transformations?
faq.a: For encoding, letters are turned into numbers, arranged in a matrix, and multiplied by a non-singular 'key' matrix to scramble them; the receiver multiplies by the inverse key to decode. For geometry, multiplying a point's position vector by a suitable matrix rotates, reflects, enlarges or shears it, and multiplying the transformation matrices together composes several transformations into one.

sections.content: Matrices are more than grids of numbers, they are single objects you can add, scale and multiply, and that packaging makes them the natural language for anything organised in rows and columns. Computer graphics rotate and scale images with them, cryptographers scramble messages with them, and economists model interlocking markets with them. This lesson builds the full HL 1.6 toolkit: the matrix operations and the conformability rule that governs multiplication, the identity matrix, determinants of 2x2 and 3x3 matrices, the inverse of a 2x2 by formula and a 3x3 by GDC, and the central application, solving a linear system as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, before finishing with encoding and geometric transformations. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples below are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice.
Matrix operations and conformability

A matrix is described by its order, rows by columns. You add or subtract two matrices only when they have the same order, combining entries in matching positions. Scalar multiplication multiplies every entry by the same number. Multiplication is the operation that surprises people: to multiply $A$ by $B$ you take rows of $A$ against columns of $B$, so the inner dimensions must match. The rule for this is called conformability.

items: **Addition / subtraction:** only for matrices of the SAME order; add entry by entry.
**Scalar multiplication:** multiply every entry by the scalar; the order is unchanged.
**Multiplication:** $AB$ exists only if the number of columns of $A$ equals the number of rows of $B$. An $(m \times n)$ times an $(n \times p)$ gives an $(m \times p)$ matrix.
**Not commutative:** in general $AB \neq BA$; sometimes only one order is even defined.

sections.content: Conformability: $(m \times n) \times (n \times p) \rightarrow (m \times p)$. \n The two INNER numbers must agree ($n = n$); the OUTER numbers give the order of the product.

Because order matters, keep the sequence of any product exactly as written. This single fact, that $AB \neq BA$, is the reason we must be careful when we later 'pre-multiply' both sides of a matrix equation by an inverse.
The identity matrix

Among square matrices, one plays the role that 1 plays for numbers: the identity matrix $I$, with 1s on the leading diagonal and 0s everywhere else. Multiplying any conformable matrix by $I$ leaves it unchanged, $AI = IA = A$. The identity is what an inverse produces: $A^{-1}$ is precisely the matrix for which $AA^{-1} = A^{-1}A = I$, so the identity is the target every inverse aims at.
The $2 \times 2$ identity: $I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, with $AI = IA = A$. \n An inverse satisfies $AA^{-1} = A^{-1}A = I$.
The determinant

Every square matrix carries a single number called its determinant, written $\det A$ or $|A|$, that decides whether the matrix can be inverted. For a $2 \times 2$ matrix you compute it by hand; for a $3 \times 3$ you use the GDC. Whatever the size, the meaning is the same: a non-zero determinant means an inverse exists, a zero determinant means it does not.
For a 2x2 matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$: \n $\det A = ad - bc$ (a scalar, not a matrix).

items: The determinant is a single number, not a matrix.
If $\det A = 0$ the matrix is **singular** and has NO inverse.
If $\det A \neq 0$ the matrix is **non-singular** and has a unique inverse.
For a $3 \times 3$ matrix, evaluate the determinant on the GDC.

sections.content: The inverse of a matrix

The inverse $A^{-1}$ undoes the effect of $A$: multiply them together, in either order, and you get the identity. For a $2 \times 2$ matrix there is a compact formula in the booklet; for a $3 \times 3$ you use the GDC. In both cases the inverse exists only when $\det A \neq 0$.
Inverse of a 2x2 matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$: \n $A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$, provided $ad - bc \neq 0$.

The 2x2 inverse formula is in your formula booklet, but the condition $\det A \neq 0$ is not written beside the answer, you must supply it. Swap the leading diagonal, negate the off-diagonal, then divide the WHOLE matrix by the determinant. For anything $3 \times 3$ or larger, go straight to the GDC: the marks are for the correct set-up and interpretation, not manual arithmetic.

sections.question: Let the matrix $P = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 5 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$. \n (a) Find $\det P$. \n (b) Hence find $P^{-1}$. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Determinant.** Using $\det P = ad - bc$:
$\det P = (4)(-3) - (-2)(5) = -12 - (-10) = -2.$  [M1 substitution]  [A1]

**(b) Inverse.** Since $\det P = -2 
eq 0$, an inverse exists. Using $P^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det P} egin{pmatrix} d & -b \ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$:
$P^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{-2} egin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \ -5 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$  [M1 substitution into formula]
$P^{-1} =  egin{pmatrix} 1.5 & -1 \ 2.5 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.$  [A1]

Either form, with the scalar left outside or multiplied in, is accepted.

sections.content: Application: solving systems of linear equations

The headline application of inverses is solving simultaneous linear equations. Line the variables up in the same order in each equation and the whole system collapses into one matrix equation $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, with $A$ the coefficients, $\mathbf{x}$ the unknowns and $\mathbf{b}$ the constants.

items: **$A$** is the square matrix of coefficients (variables in a fixed order).
**$\mathbf{x}$** is the column of unknowns, e.g. $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix}$.
**$\mathbf{b}$** is the column of constants from the right-hand sides.

sections.content: You cannot divide by a matrix, so instead pre-multiply both sides by $A^{-1}$. Because $A^{-1}A = I$, this isolates $\mathbf{x}$.

$A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \ \Rightarrow\ A^{-1}(A\mathbf{x}) = A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \ \Rightarrow\ (A^{-1}A)\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \ \Rightarrow\ I\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}.$

sections.solution:
Write the system as $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with the variables in the order $c, v, r$:
$A =  egin{pmatrix} 10 & 5 & 8 \ 12 & 7 & 6 \ 8 & 10 & 5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x} =  egin{pmatrix} c \ v \ r \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} =  egin{pmatrix} 350 \ 390 \ 395 \end{pmatrix}.$  [M1 correct $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ set-up]  [A1 correct $A$, $\mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{b}$]

Solve by $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Enter $A$ and $\mathbf{b}$ on the GDC and compute $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.  [M1 intent to use $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$]

The GDC returns:
$\mathbf{x} =  egin{pmatrix} 15 \ 20 \ 12.5 \end{pmatrix}.$  [A2 all three values correct]

So a Chocolate cake costs £15, a Vanilla cake £20 and a Red Velvet cake £12.50.  [A1 interpretation in context]

sections.content: For a system-of-equations question you MUST show the set-up: write the matrix equation $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ (and the equations themselves if they are not given). Those lines earn the method marks even if you mistype a number into the GDC. A bare final answer copied off the calculator will not score full marks.
Further applications: encoding and transformations

Encoding a message uses the inverse as a decoding key. Letters are converted to numbers and arranged in a matrix, then multiplied by a non-singular key matrix to scramble them; the recipient multiplies the scrambled matrix by the inverse key to recover the original. The determinant must be non-zero, otherwise the scramble cannot be undone. Geometric transformations use matrices differently: multiplying a point's position vector by a suitable matrix maps it to a new point, so rotations, reflections, enlargements and shears each have a matrix, and composing transformations corresponds to multiplying their matrices (in reverse order of application). The determinant of a transformation matrix even carries meaning, its magnitude is the area scale factor of the transformation.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Assuming $AB = BA$** — matrix multiplication is not commutative. Keep every product in the order written; reversing it usually changes the answer or makes the product undefined.
**Ignoring conformability** — before multiplying, check the inner dimensions match. An $(m \times n)$ can only multiply an $(n \times p)$; mismatched inner numbers mean no product exists.
**Mis-stating the determinant** — for a $2 \times 2$ it is $ad - bc$, not $ab - cd$ or $ad + bc$. A sign or pairing slip here poisons the inverse and everything after it.
**Dropping the $\frac{1}{\det A}$ factor** — in the 2x2 inverse you must divide the swapped-and-negated matrix by the determinant; forgetting it leaves you with a matrix that is not the inverse.

**Solving in the wrong order** — $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, never $\mathbf{b}A^{-1}$. The inverse goes on the LEFT of the constants; the reversed product is not even conformable for a column $\mathbf{b}$.
**Inverting a singular matrix** — if $\det A = 0$ there is no inverse, so $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has no unique solution. Do not report a 'solution'; state that the matrix is singular.
**Writing only the GDC answer** — for a system you must show the $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ set-up to earn the method marks; a lone final answer risks most of them.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

On Paper 1 and Paper 2 alike the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working, a method mark (M) or an accuracy mark (A), and an accuracy mark depends on the method mark it follows. Follow-through (FT) means a value that is wrong earlier does not have to cost you the marks that depend on it, provided the later step is done correctly on your own figure. The engine also ignores subsequent working (ISW) once a correct answer appears, and accepts any equivalent form and any correctly-rounded value, but only if the method is on the page. The GDC is expected for a $3 \times 3$; a $2 \times 2$ inverse should be shown by formula. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: Given $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$, find $\det A$ and $A^{-1}$, then solve $A\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 10 \end{pmatrix}$. [5]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Determinant.** Using $\det A = ad - bc$:
$\det A = (2)(4) - (1)(3) = 8 - 3 = 5.$

**Inverse.** Since $\det A = 5 
eq 0$, an inverse exists. Using the 2x2 formula:
$A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}.$

**Solve.** With $\mathbf{b} =  egin{pmatrix} 5 \ 10 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$:
$\mathbf{x} = \dfrac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix} egin{pmatrix} 5 \ 10 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 4(5) + (-1)(10) \ (-3)(5) + 2(10) \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 10 \ 5 \end{pmatrix} =  egin{pmatrix} 2 \ 1 \end{pmatrix}.$

**Conclusion:** $\det A = 5$, $A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, and $\mathbf{x} =  egin{pmatrix} 2 \ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, i.e. $x = 2$, $y = 1$.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 5 marks:**

- **M1 — determinant method.** Awarded for correctly applying $ad - bc$, i.e. writing $(2)(4) - (1)(3)$. It is the method that is rewarded, so it survives an arithmetic slip in the next line.
- **A1 — determinant value.** Awarded for $\det A = 5$. This accuracy mark depends on the M1 above.
- **M1 — inverse method.** Awarded for correct use of the 2x2 inverse formula: swapping the leading diagonal, negating the off-diagonal and dividing by the determinant, i.e. $ rac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ in structure.
- **A1 — inverse.** Awarded for the correct $A^{-1} =  rac{1}{5} egin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ (or the equivalent with the scalar multiplied in). FT applies from the determinant: a candidate with a wrong $\det A$ who forms the inverse correctly on their own value still earns this.
- **A1 — solution.** Awarded for $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b} =  egin{pmatrix} 2 \ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, computed in the correct order. This is FT on the candidate's own inverse: a student whose inverse differed but who multiplied $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ correctly keeps the mark.

**'Accept equivalent forms and correct rounding.'** The engine accepts $A^{-1}$ with the $ rac{1}{5}$ left outside or multiplied in, and accepts the answer written as a column $ egin{pmatrix} 2 \ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ or as $x = 2$, $y = 1$. Once the correct $\mathbf{x}$ appears, ISW means later restatements do not lose marks.

**Bottom line:** two of the five marks are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, and the accuracy marks are shielded by follow-through, so a student who writes only $\mathbf{x} =  egin{pmatrix} 2 \ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ with no determinant, inverse or order shown risks losing 3-4 marks, while one who shows every line keeps the method even when a number slips.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way, analytically, mark by mark, with method marks, dependent accuracy marks and follow-through. So always show the method: write $\det A = ad - bc$ and its value, quote the 2x2 inverse formula (or state you used the GDC for a $3 \times 3$), set the problem up as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$, and compute $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ in the correct order. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

Matrices are the backbone of the rest of the HL number-and-algebra strand and beyond. The eigenvalues and eigenvectors you meet later are built directly on determinants and the equation $A\mathbf{x}=\lambda\mathbf{x}$; the transition matrices of Markov chains multiply exactly as here, with the long-run state read from a special vector; and systems of differential equations are solved by diagonalising a coefficient matrix. Even the geometric transformations touched on above return when you model repeated processes or graphics. Master the three moves of this lesson, check conformability, test the determinant, and solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ with the inverse in the correct order, and the more advanced matrix topics become variations on a method you already own.

sections.label: Get a matrix question marked: find the determinant and inverse, then solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ with full working
